1: The heading of the Philistia oracle — before Pharaoh attacked Gaza. The timing note roots the oracle in a specific historical moment while pointing beyond it. Philistia represents the ancient covenantal enemy of Israel, recurring across the entire biblical narrative from Joshua through David. The final defeat of the Philistines in Jeremiah's oracle anticipates their complete disappearance from history — they cease as a distinct people, absorbed by the nations. Christ fulfills the pattern: the enemies of God's people are ultimately not merely defeated but dissolved, while God's people remain (Romans 9:6 — "not all who are descended from Israel are Israel").
2: "Waters rising from the north, becoming an overflowing flood." The flood-judgment from the north (Babylon) using the Nile-flood imagery against the coastal peoples. Isaiah 8:7-8 uses the same overflowing-river image for the Assyrian advance: "the LORD is about to bring against them the mighty floodwaters of the Euphrates... it will overflow all its channels and sweep on." Revelation 12:15-16 — the dragon pours water like a flood from his mouth, but the earth opens and swallows the flood. Christ defeats the flood-judgment that sweeps nations away: he calmed the sea (Mark 4:39) and in doing so demonstrated his authority over the flood-powers of judgment.
3: "At the thunder of hoofs of his horses and the rattling of his chariots, fathers will not turn back for their children." The terror so overwhelming it breaks the strongest family bond. Luke 21:16 — "you will be betrayed even by parents, brothers, sisters, relatives, and friends." Matthew 10:37 — "anyone who loves their father or mother more than me is not worthy of me." The family-bond-breaking terror of Jeremiah 47:3 becomes in Christ's teaching the test of ultimate loyalty: the one who holds family bonds above Christ loses him; the one who holds Christ above all gains everything (Mark 10:29-30).
4: "The day has come to lay waste all the Philistines and to cut off from Tyre and Sidon every remaining ally." The cutting-off of every support system — total isolation. Mark 14:50 — "everyone deserted him and fled." At the cross, Christ's every ally was cut off — his disciples fled, Judas betrayed, Peter denied. The total isolation of Philistia in judgment prefigures the total abandonment Christ experienced at Calvary — but his isolation was not the end of judgment but the beginning of salvation.
5: "Gaza is shaved bald; Ashkelon is destroyed. O remnant of their valley, how long will you slash yourself in grief?" The mourning rituals of the defeated — shaved heads, slashed skin. Isaiah 50:6 — "I offered my back to those who beat me, my cheeks to those who pulled out my beard; I did not hide my face from mocking and spitting." The humiliation of the coastland peoples (Gaza, Ashkelon) is answered by the voluntary humiliation of Christ — but he does not slash himself in grief; he submits to others' violence. His humiliation is not the humiliation of defeat but the descent that makes exaltation possible (Philippians 2:8-9).
6: "O sword of the LORD, how long before you rest? Return to your sheath; be still and stop." The prophet crying out for the divine sword to cease — the anguish of one who speaks the word of judgment and longs for it to end. Revelation 6:10 — the souls under the altar cry out: "how long, Sovereign Lord, holy and true, until you judge the inhabitants of the earth and avenge our blood?" The "how long?" of the prophet in Jeremiah 47:6 and the "how long?" of the martyrs in Revelation 6:10 are bookends of the same question. Christ answers both: his return brings both the completion of judgment and the end of the waiting.
7: "But how can it be still when the LORD has given it its charge? Against Ashkelon and the seacoast — there he has set it." The sword cannot rest because it has been given a divine assignment. John 19:11 — "you would have no power over me if it were not given to you from above." The Romans who executed Jesus wielded the sword that had been given its charge — the cross was not a human accident but a divinely appointed execution of the covenant curse that Christ bore. Acts 4:27-28 — "they did what your power and will had decided beforehand should happen." The sword given its charge in Jeremiah 47:7 finds its most profound application at Calvary.
